instructions.instruction: Use imperative mood in the description (e.g. 'add' not 'added')
instructions.original_snippets: Description: imperative mood (`add` not `added`), lowercase, no trailing period, ≤ 72 characters
instructions.relevant_when: Writing any commit message description/header

instructions.instruction: Keep the description lowercase
instructions.original_snippets: Description: imperative mood (`add` not `added`), lowercase, no trailing period, ≤ 72 characters
instructions.relevant_when: Writing any commit message description/header

instructions.instruction: No trailing period at the end of the description
instructions.original_snippets: Description: imperative mood (`add` not `added`), lowercase, no trailing period, ≤ 72 characters
instructions.relevant_when: Writing any commit message description/header

instructions.instruction: Keep the header (type + scope + description) to ≤ 72 characters
instructions.original_snippets: Description: imperative mood (`add` not `added`), lowercase, no trailing period, ≤ 72 characters...NEVER exceed 72 characters in the header
instructions.relevant_when: Writing any commit message, especially for complex features

instructions.instruction: Separate the body from the header with one blank line
instructions.original_snippets: Body: separated by one blank line, lines wrapped at 72 characters, explains *why* not *what*
instructions.relevant_when: Writing commit messages with a body section

instructions.instruction: Wrap body lines at 72 characters
instructions.original_snippets: Body: separated by one blank line, lines wrapped at 72 characters, explains *why* not *what*
instructions.relevant_when: Writing commit messages with a multi-line body

instructions.instruction: The body should explain *why* not *what* (the code already shows what)
instructions.original_snippets: Body: separated by one blank line, lines wrapped at 72 characters, explains *why* not *what*...The body explains *why* — the code already shows *what*.
instructions.relevant_when: Writing commit messages with a body section

instructions.instruction: Use footers in Token: value format (e.g. 'Closes: #123', 'BREAKING CHANGE: description')
instructions.original_snippets: Footers: `Token: value` format (e.g. `Closes: #123`, `BREAKING CHANGE: description`)
instructions.relevant_when: Referencing issues, PRs, or documenting breaking changes

instructions.instruction: Use 'feat' type for new features or user-facing capabilities — triggers MINOR version bump
instructions.original_snippets: | `feat` | MINOR (1.0.0 → 1.1.0) | New feature or user-facing capability |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing a new user-facing feature

instructions.instruction: Use 'fix' type for bug fixes or incorrect behavior — triggers PATCH version bump
instructions.original_snippets: | `fix` | PATCH (1.0.0 → 1.0.1) | Bug fix or incorrect behavior |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing a bug fix

instructions.instruction: Use 'refactor' type for restructuring with the same behavior (no SemVer impact)
instructions.original_snippets: | `refactor` | None | Restructuring, same behavior |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing code restructuring without behavior changes

instructions.instruction: Use 'docs' type for documentation-only changes
instructions.original_snippets: | `docs` | None | Documentation only |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing documentation changes

instructions.instruction: Use 'ci' type for CI/CD configuration changes
instructions.original_snippets: | `ci` | None | CI/CD configuration changes |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing CI pipeline or configuration changes

instructions.instruction: Use 'build' type for build system or dependency changes
instructions.original_snippets: | `build` | None | Build system or dependency changes |
instructions.relevant_when: Updating build tooling, package.json dependencies, etc.

instructions.instruction: Use 'perf' type for measurable performance improvements
instructions.original_snippets: | `perf` | None | Measurable performance improvement |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing performance optimizations

instructions.instruction: Use 'style' type for formatting changes with no logic change
instructions.original_snippets: | `style` | None | Code formatting, no logic change |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing formatting-only changes

instructions.instruction: Use 'test' type for adding or updating tests
instructions.original_snippets: | `test` | None | Adding or updating tests |
instructions.relevant_when: Committing test additions or modifications

instructions.instruction: Use 'chore' only for maintenance tasks not covered by other types (e.g. bumping lock files); never use it as a catch-all
instructions.original_snippets: NEVER use `chore` as a catch-all...`chore` is for maintenance tasks not covered by other types (e.g., bumping lock files). Using it for features, refactors, or docs hides semantic meaning from changelog generators.
instructions.relevant_when: Categorizing miscellaneous changes that don't obviously fit another type

instructions.instruction: Append '!' after type/scope for breaking changes (e.g. 'feat!:') to trigger MAJOR version bump
instructions.original_snippets: Breaking changes: append `!` after type/scope (`feat!:`) and/or add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer — triggers a MAJOR version bump.
instructions.relevant_when: Committing changes that break the public API or require migration

instructions.instruction: Always include a 'BREAKING CHANGE:' footer (not just '!') when the API surface changes, with migration description
instructions.original_snippets: NEVER omit `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer when the API surface changes...The `!` suffix alone is not sufficient for all tooling. `BREAKING CHANGE:` in the footer carries the migration description that changelog generators parse for release notes.
instructions.relevant_when: Committing any breaking API or interface change

instructions.instruction: Do not put implementation details in the header; they belong in the body
instructions.original_snippets: NEVER put implementation details in the header...The header is the commit summary visible in `git log --oneline`. Implementation details belong in the body.
instructions.relevant_when: Writing commit headers for technically complex changes

instructions.instruction: Write the header for the person reviewing a CHANGELOG six months from now, not for yourself today
instructions.original_snippets: Write the header for the person reviewing a CHANGELOG six months from now, not for yourself today.
instructions.relevant_when: Choosing wording for any commit message header

instructions.instruction: Never use past tense in the description
instructions.original_snippets: NEVER use past tense in the description...The header completes the sentence 'If applied, this commit will…' — past tense breaks that contract
instructions.relevant_when: Writing any commit message description
